hook: The bronze ancestor of Wroclaw's 1,000-strong dwarf army, born from anti-communist graffiti
facts: Installed 2001 on Swidnicka Street to honour the Orange Alternative movement
The original dwarves were painted on walls in 1982 by Waldemar 'Major' Fydrych over censors' paint stains
Largest of the city's dwarves; over 1,000 now scattered citywide
Swidnicka hosted most of the Orange Alternative's absurdist street happenings
w: Where the dwarf army began. In 1982, activist 'Major' Fydrych painted little gnomes over the smudges censors left where they'd blotted out anti-communist slogans, mocking the regime into absurdity. This bronze patriarch landed in 2001; his thousand-odd progeny now hide on every kerb. Resistance, in a pointy hat.

hook: Two tiny bronze dwarves doomed to roll a granite ball forever
facts: Placed at Swidnicka 1, near the old town hall, in 2005
Named for Sisyphus, condemned in Greek myth to push a boulder uphill eternally
Among the most photographed of Wroclaw's dwarves
Stand on Swidnicka, the Orange Alternative's old happening ground
w: Two thumb-high bronze dwarves heave a granite ball that never crests, named for the Greek king condemned to roll his boulder uphill for eternity. Placed on Swidnicka in 2005, they sweat through the same hopeless shove while tourists crouch to pat them. Mythology's cruellest punishment, rendered adorable.

hook: A footbridge slung between two church towers, scene of grim trials and a sweeping ghost
facts: Connects the two towers of St Mary Magdalene Church, first recorded 1459
Towers stand around 45 metres high; reached by 247 steps
Now a public viewpoint over the Old Town
Two dwarf figures on the rail represent the legend's Tekla and Martynka
legend: Legend says accused witches were made to walk the parapet; those who fell were innocent
Legend says a vain girl, Tekla, is cursed to sweep the bridge for eternity
w: A slim footbridge strung 45 metres up between the twin towers of St Mary Magdalene, recorded since 1459. Legend says accused witches walked the parapet here, the fallen judged innocent, the steady ones burned. Another tale curses a vain girl to sweep the planks forever. Climb 247 steps for the view she can't escape.

hook: A cathedral island still lit by hand each dusk by a caped lamplighter
facts: Around 100 butane gas lanterns lit and snuffed by hand daily
Gas lighting first appeared in the city in 1846; these survived 1960s electrification
The lamplighter appears 20-30 minutes before sunset in cape and top hat
Only Wroclaw and Zagreb keep working lamplighters in the EU
w: On the old cathedral island, dusk still arrives by hand. A lamplighter in black cape and top hat works the cobbled lanes around the Tumski Bridge, touching a long pole to roughly a hundred gas lanterns, then snuffing them before dawn. Gaslight came here in 1846 and simply never left. Only Zagreb keeps the trade too.

hook: A 114-metre painting that wraps you inside an 1894 battle scene
facts: Cycloramic canvas 15 by 114 metres depicting the 1794 Battle of Raclawice
Painted 1893-94 by Jan Styka, Wojciech Kossak and a team, originally for Lwow
Brought to Wroclaw after WWII with the Ossolineum collection
Communist authorities found it sensitive; shown only from 1985
w: Step into a purpose-built rotunda and the painting swallows you: 114 metres of scythe-wielding peasants and Kosciuszko's 1794 charge, 15 metres tall, the foreground melting into real earth and props. Painted for Lwow in 1894, hauled west after the war, then hidden by nervous communists until 1985. Battle as 360-degree spell.

hook: A pioneering 1913 concrete dome that quietly rewrote what buildings could do
facts: Designed by Max Berg, built 1911-13 for the centenary of the 1813 anti-Napoleonic rising
Reinforced-concrete ribbed dome 65 metres across, among the largest of its day
Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2006
Still hosts concerts, sport and conferences
w: Max Berg's 1913 dome looks dour from outside, then you step under 65 metres of bare ribbed concrete and grasp why engineers still make pilgrimages. Built for a Napoleonic-rising centenary, it pushed reinforced concrete further than anyone had dared. UNESCO listed in 2006. Modernism's blunt, astonishing prototype.

hook: Poelzig's four-domed 1913 pavilion, now full of difficult Polish art
facts: Designed by Hans Poelzig, built 1912-13 for the Centennial Exhibition
Four symmetrical domes around an inner courtyard, early concrete-frame modernism
Houses the Museum of Contemporary Art (National Museum branch) since 2016
Shows post-war Polish art including Abakanowicz and Althamer
w: Hans Poelzig's restrained sibling to the Centennial Hall, four low domes ringing a courtyard, raised in 1913 and quietly radical for its concrete frame. Since 2016 it cradles the National Museum's contemporary collection: Abakanowicz's woven hulks, Althamer's unsettling figures. Old exhibition hall, new disquiet.

n: Jas i Malgosia (Hansel and Gretel Houses)
hook: Two little houses holding hands through a baroque gate of the dead
facts: Survivors of a row of narrow houses that ringed St Elizabeth's churchyard
Linked by a baroque archway rebuilt 1728, once the cemetery gate
Gate bears the Latin 'Mors ianua vitae' (death is the gate of life)
Nicknamed after the Grimm tale only after WWII
w: Two slim townhouses lean toward each other and clasp hands across a baroque arch, so locals named them after Hansel and Gretel. Less cosy up close: the arch once closed off St Elizabeth's graveyard, and its Latin motto reads 'death opens onto life'. Once home to the lowliest clergy. A fairy tale built over bones.

hook: A neoclassical synagogue saved by its own courtyard on Kristallnacht
facts: Built 1827-29 to a design by Carl Ferdinand Langhans
Survived Kristallnacht in 1938 because burning it risked neighbouring buildings
Its courtyard became a deportation collection point from 1943
Restored and rededicated in 2010 as the Jewish community's cultural centre
w: Tucked in a courtyard off Wlodkowica, this neoclassical synagogue of 1829 survived Kristallnacht for a chilling reason: torching it might have spread fire to non-Jewish buildings beside it. Spared, then defiled, its yard became an assembly point for deportations. Restored in 2010, it sings again. Survival by grim accident.

hook: A hidden courtyard where rescued communist-era neon signs glow again
facts: Open-air gallery of restored 1960s-70s neons, created 2014 at Ruska 46C
The courtyard once held 'Reklama', the region's biggest neon maker
Includes the old Wroclaw Glowny station and Kino Warszawa signs
Free to visit; best after dark
w: Duck through the arch at Ruska 46 and a scruffy courtyard flickers to life with salvaged communist-era neon: a railway-station sign, a cinema's cursive, shop logos rescued from skips. Fittingly, the yard once housed the region's biggest neon workshop, so the signs came home. Free, and best when the dark lets them buzz.

hook: A water museum inside a Victorian underground reservoir
facts: Opened 2015 in a 19th-century covered drinking-water tank
The reservoir, Filter No. V, was built 1890-93 under an earth embankment
Held the city's clean water until 2011, then was converted
Interactive exhibits on water in nature, history and technology
w: Beneath a grassy embankment hides a cathedral of water: a covered reservoir of 1893 that fed the city its drinking supply until 2011. Drained and reborn in 2015, the brick vaults now hold an exhibition on everything wet, from deep-sea pressure to medieval plumbing. A tank that quenched the city, then explained itself.

hook: A vast fountain that throws water screens for laser shows behind Centennial Hall
facts: Unveiled 2009, marking 20 years since Poland's first free post-war elections
Covers about a hectare with 300-plus nozzles and 800 lights
Jets reach 40 metres; forms a water screen for projections
Runs only in the warm season, with free evening shows
w: Behind the Centennial Hall's pergola, a hectare of water erupts on cue: 300 nozzles, jets to 40 metres, and a curtain of spray that doubles as a projection screen for laser-lit shows set to music. Unveiled in 2009 to mark twenty years since Poland's first free vote. Summer evenings only, then it sleeps through winter.

hook: A baroque assembly hall that drips with gilt, fresco and stucco
facts: Built 1728-32 in the University main building; consecrated 1732
Named for Emperor Leopold I, who founded the university in 1702
Ceiling frescoes by Johann Christoph Handke, sculpture by Franz Joseph Mangoldt
Open to visitors via the University of Wroclaw Museum
w: Up a plain university staircase waits a baroque detonation: an assembly hall of 1732 where frescoed sky, gilded saints and writhing stucco leave barely a calm square inch. Named for the Habsburg emperor who founded the place, it survived the war's flames. Among the finest baroque interiors anywhere east of Vienna. Look up.

hook: An old observatory tower, the only one of three the university could afford
facts: Established 1791 as an observatory by ex-Jesuit Longinus Anton Jungnitz
Named 'Mathematical' as the sole tower built of three planned
Once held a meridian line fixing solar noon to seconds
Terrace at about 42 metres gives Old Town views
w: The university dreamed of three grand towers; the money ran out after one, so this lone stub got the consolation name 'Mathematical'. From 1791 an ex-Jesuit ran an observatory up here, a meridian line and ceiling aperture pinning solar noon to the second. Climb to the 42-metre terrace for the Old Town spread below. Thrift with a view.

hook: The world's only zoo pavilion devoted entirely to Africa's waters
facts: Opened 2014 at Wroclaw Zoo, Poland's oldest zoo (founded 1865)
The only facility worldwide dedicated solely to African aquatic life
Around 15 million litres of water across some 21 pools
Holds hippos, manatees, penguins, crocodiles and 350-plus fish species
w: Inside Poland's oldest zoo sits a stranger idea: a pavilion that follows water across one continent, from a Mozambique reef to a Nile crocodile pool, holding some 15 million litres. Opened in 2014, the only place on earth to stage Africa's aquatic life this way. Hippos overhead, penguins underfoot. A continent, plumbed.

hook: A surviving communist-era milk bar still ladling cheap pierogi
facts: Milk bar (bar mleczny) on Kuznicza 48, reportedly open since around 1970
Milk bars were state-subsidised cheap canteens of the PRL era
Serves pierogi, soups and pancakes at low prices
Open weekdays and Saturday, closed Sunday
w: A relic of the People's Republic that refuses to die: a milk bar, the state-subsidised canteen where workers once ate for coins. Trays, terse staff, steam, and pierogi cheaper than your coffee, served on Kuznicza since around 1970. The name nods to a cult comedy about exactly this kind of place. Communist comfort food, still warm.

hook: A round Nazi air-raid bunker reborn as a contemporary art museum
facts: Cylindrical reinforced-concrete shelter built 1942 by architect Richard Konwiarz
About 31 metres across, six storeys, walls over a metre thick
Served as a fortress hospital during the 1945 siege of Breslau
Houses Muzeum Wspolczesne Wroclaw since 2011
w: A squat concrete drum, six storeys of metre-thick wall, built in 1942 to shield civilians and pressed into service as a fortress hospital while Breslau burned around it in 1945. The blocks nearby were flattened; the bunker shrugged. Since 2011 its raw cells hold contemporary art. War's ugliest box, now a gallery.

hook: A fortress mound crowned by a colonnaded belvedere, lately rescued from ruin
facts: Fragment of the old city fortifications, a raised belvedere of 1867
Designed by Karl Schmidt; named Liebichshohe after its sponsors
Arched colonnade, fountain and a domed watchtower with Victoria statue
Fell derelict, then fully restored 2022-24
w: On a bastion of the vanished city walls, two brothers built a pleasure-mound in 1867: a curved colonnade, a fountain, a domed watchtower topped by winged Victoria. For decades it crumbled, graffitied and fenced, a romantic wreck. A 2022-24 rescue gave it back its splendour. Faded grandeur, recently un-faded.

hook: Bronze pedestrians who sink into the pavement on one side and rise on the other
facts: Installed 2005 by sculptor Jerzy Kalina at Swidnicka and Pilsudskiego
Fourteen life-sized figures, seven descending, seven emerging
Marks the anniversary of 1981 martial law in communist Poland
Reads as people vanishing underground, then resurfacing
w: At a busy crossing, seven bronze figures trudge into the pavement and sink from sight; across the road, seven more surface on the far kerb. Sculptor Jerzy Kalina set them there in 2005 for the anniversary of martial law, when people simply disappeared and, sometimes, came back. A whole nation crossing underground. Stop and count them.
